text: So I am a psychologist and I study achievement.
Most psychologists who study achievement study intelligence.
And I think that if the last talk didn't convince you and I have a suspicion you didn't

Maybe a very small part of the story.
And it is, in fact, possible that we even have that small part of the story wrong in terms

Something that is relatively immutable over the course of one's life.
But I can't do a study of all the other things than intelligence.
Everything else that made up achievement.
It kind of a circuitous route.

I turned to my left and turned to my right and everybody else was drinking, capace, and

probably necessary ingredients of high achievement in any field that you want to consider.
So what I did between the age of 22 and 32 was, well many different things, all of which I think

See, I was a McKinsey consultant.

So I started graduate school in 2002.
I have three more years on my clock which means many things among which means I can't give

Psychologists have been interested in the distinction between talent and everything else for years.
So before we had words to describe it, we were also probably interested in it.
But here's a quote from Clark Hall, one of the eminent Americans' psychologists of the early
He wrote a little review.
He reviewed the literature that was out there which was quite easy to do in 1928.
There was a whole lot less of it.
He said, you know there's really two things.
There's our talent and I would emphasize what Chris said.
Talent is multifaceted.
There's creativity, there's visual creativity, that's different from musical creativity.
There's analytical talent, there's athletic talent, there's musical talent.
But let's put them all in one category.
There's intelligence that's conventionally defined.
Then there are all of those many things that are so much worse understood in a way.
All the capacities that allow us to unlock our talents.
He would put those in the category of industry.
William James made the same distinction.
William James wrote a famous essay in 1907 called The Energy of Men.
And William James, who arguably is the founder of American psychology, said that there are our
talents in those things that unlock our talents.
And we could design all of psychology to try to understand these two things.
I would argue that we've done some amount of work on the talents and almost nothing on the unlocking.
When I considered what is it that unlocks people's potential, what enables people to become
a world-class musician, a world-class teacher, a world-class performer.
I struggled with this word to call what I was becoming to understand was one of these key ingredients.
Eventually I called it grit, which I named in part after the somewhat mediocre western that John Wayne
And I'll say a little bit more about that.
But the reason why I came to this concept of grit was I interviewed people that I knew that were
at the tops of their fields.
So it was relatively opportunistic.
I mean I interviewed my friend who would want to MacArthur.
I interviewed investment bankers who at least at that time were very successful.
Interviewed musicians and professors and the like.
And people would often say, the people who are the top in my field, they're the really talented ones.
But just as often, in fact I would say more often people said that these individuals, that
the top of their fields, had this kind of tenacious, doggy, perseverance.
Even like anyone else that they knew and it was actually that which vaulted them to the top.
So I called it true grit after this movie which is really about a young girl from Yale County
Arkansas who like in typical western form her father is unjustly murdered.
She spends the rest of the movie, avenging his death.
And Rester Cogburn plays the one-eyed son, my alcoholic sheriff who follows her along.
And everyone thinks the true grit is really about John Wayne of course.
And it's really about this young girl who against all odds pursues a very long term,
almost impossible goal.
And eventually, eventually with the emphasis on eventually, it succeeds in that goal.
And this is the quality that I study.
Charles Darwin had a half-cousin named Francis Galton and they shared a correspondence.
I like to think that correspondence today is as a rich and personally revealing as a
poet was when you had to put a pen to paper.
So maybe if they had email they would have shared the same kinds of conversations.
This conversation, this quote, this is actually the letter on the left and maybe a little more
legible on the right, was Charles Darwin's response to Francis Galton who had written a book
called hereditary genius.
Francis Galton made the claim that genius had three parts, one part talent, one part passion or
zeal and one part hard work.
Charles Darwin's response to that was, you know, that's a really interesting idea.
I thought it was all the hard work and the passion.
Maybe there was a role for talent, after all.
Charles Darwin himself didn't actually consider his intellect to be at all special.
He thought he had quite ordinary mind, but a very specific interest and focus and a lot of zeal
and hard work.
Moving up in a little closer to where we are in time, there was a graduate student at Stanford
named Katherine Cox.
She was the graduate student of a professor there named Lewis Termin.
He gave us possibly the most widely used intelligence test today, the Stanford Benei IQ test.
So she was doing her graduate work in a lab where everybody studied intelligence and had

Katherine took a very different take on her own research.
She wanted to know what are these other qualities that make for genius, that make for
People were actually going to do something in the world.
She read the biographies of 300 well-known geniuses and she isolated a few qualities which really
distinguished the geniuses who made a mark on the world.
One of them was the tendency not to abandon tasks from mere changeability in her words.
In other words, not being a dilatant, not being a flick, not being me from the age of 22 to 32.
I sort of, you know, from one award to another, from one career to another, never actually
setting sites on a port that I was going to consistently work towards.
And I think we know many, many extremely bright people who don't have the capacity to stay
on task towards one goal and keeps switching from one to the other.
I teach at Penn, I see hundreds and thousands of kids pass through Penn's Ivy League portals
and they have this conception that essentially when they go off into the world, it will
be an okay and good strategy to go to law school.
And if I don't like a law, I, you know, filled my pre-med requirement so I could go back and
do medical school and if I don't like that, there's always management consulting, the fallback
of any Ivy League graduate.
What I want to tell them is that history and psychology tell us that changing around a lot is
actually not a good way to get anywhere.
The other quality that she isolated in her work and her sort of reading of biographies was
probably more predictable.
I think many teachers and many, even many kids might recognize that you know, having perseverance
in the face of adversity, you know, setbacks, failures, that's important.
And it's the combination of those things that I call grit.
So it's this stamina quality, not just being passionate, but sustaining that passion for a long

Setbacks don't disappoint me.
I finish whatever I begin.
I am determined.

The first study I want to tell you about was that done at West Point Military Academy.
The first summer when you go to West Point is called Beast Barrax.
So you show up, they check you for tattoos, can't have a tattoo if you go to West Point.
They shave your hair and they set you down and you take a very, very long battery of psychological
and intelligence tests.
So I slipped in the grit scale on this second day of training for a group of cadets.
And like many other psychologists had my battery of measures kind of hoping that I would
be able to predict something over and beyond what else was being collected at West Point.
West Point has been collecting data for many, many years on what predicts survival through
So they lose a good number of their cadets every summer that they do this, the first year
cadets, even though they tried to select the people who obviously were not going to drop
So here are the results.
Great is the dark blue line and essentially how to read this graph is that on the left is the

Barrax and on the x-axis is what quartile you're in.
So at the far right hand here we have people on the top quartile on grit scores, 96% of those
cadets actually stayed through the summer.
You can see essentially that there is this positive relationship, more grit and more likely
Here is the whole candidate score.
This is a weighted average of your SAT, your GPA, how many push ups you can do, literally.
And you can see that it's actually true that if you're in the bottom 25% of their whole candidate

But isn't it interesting that the top 25% of people on this score, which West Point has been
many years and lots of your tax dollars, trying to figure out the best predictor of

And self-discipline, which is being able to resist temptation, it's also an important quality,

Very good quality when it comes to staying on your diet and doing your homework, not such

It seems to be predictive as well, not quite as predictive when you run the statistics
We replicated the study every single year in the last five years at West Point Military Academy,

or in their special forces officers or in their Navy SEALs or in their Air Force cadets.
But the point here is that grit is predicting something that the people who stay in a very
very challenging environment are not just the very talented ones, it's something else.
In fact, in this study and in every study that I've run since then, I was looking to see whether
the gritty people were the ones who were the talented ones.
Maybe when you're really good at something, it makes you stay in.
In fact, we find quite the opposite.
At West Point and elsewhere, we find that the gritty people on measures of talent have less.
So it's by no means a guarantee of grit that you actually start off as one of the gifted.

Of the National Spelling Bee Kids.

In a follow-up study to this one, we investigated why is it that gritty kids are winning the
So we recruited another sample of kids from the following year Spelling Bee.
We sent them surveys.
We measured their grit on self-report questionnaires.
But then we asked them very detailed questions about what they did.
So it turns out that kids who are in the National Spelling Bee competition, you know,
they're studying anywhere from, you know, an hour a week to, scarily 35 or 40 hours a week.
But what differentiates kids who are gritty from kids who are not gritty is not just the hours
of work that they're putting in.
They're putting the hardest kind of work in.
They're not studying the words that they already know.
They're not sitting around being quizzed on what's already pretty much coming easily.
They isolate what they don't know.
They identify their own weaknesses and then they work just on that.
And that seems to be characteristic of high achievement and of what grit enables you to do.
It's basically being a very uncomfortable place for some part of your day, working extremely hard
and then to get up and do it all over again and again and again and again.
There's a graph that goes with this 10-year rule that I mentioned at the beginning of the talk.
This is the deliberate practice graph.
This graph actually accurately describes the rise of skill, the gain and skill over time
for really just about any domain that's been studied.
Even Mozart, who somewhat argue, is proof of concept for genius.
Mozart must have been born as great as he was because who else could have been composing melodies
that were still listening to at the age of five or six.
It turns out that Mozart also fits this graph, but he was doing probably eight hours of deliberate
practice a day from as early as he could sit up, whereas most world class performers only
Mozart had already accumulated basically 10,000 hours of deliberate practice.
Here's the interesting thing of the graph.
It's really 10 years since you started to discipline until you get to world class peak performance.
What's another interesting point about this, which you can't see from this graph, is that
most people do this.
They don't have the grit to essentially sustain this deliberate practice over all this

I think that essentially the question for the Blumen School and for the rest of us who are interested
